item.title: The secret to a successful marriage? Non-lethal projectiles, of course!
item.description: The hubs and I are approaching our 16th dating anniversary (married for 5yrs). It definitely isn’t always nerf gun fights and laughter, but doing stupid shit like this together really does make the day-to-day a lot more fun and interesting! *EDIT* — wow! Most viral! Happy you guys got a kick out of this! 10/10 will do again. Hope everyone is keeping well! Enjoy your days!

item.title: Keshia Thomas is a hero
item.description: Keshia Thomas is the main character of this story. I don't care what happened to this guy, whether he apologized or not doesn't change who we are talking about. She matters, her actions were those of a hero, a person who put their moral code above petty feelings of judgement or superiority. She lead by example. Bravo. Keshia Thomas is an African-American woman known for a 1996 event at which she was photographed protecting a man believed to have been a Ku Klux Klan supporter. The resulting photograph, which was taken by Mark Brunner, has been considered to be iconic in nature and was named one of Life magazine's "Pictures of the Year" for 1996. I cannot imagine having that level of goodness. She is a total badass.

item.title: This article FYW
item.description:
looks like Omicron caught up with us
Yep...
I got that throat hurt that sounds sexy
Always tired Always anxious waiting on the ego to take over
Thankfully not feeling like the 8mile song
Most *VIRAL* indeed... meant for the title to say FTW as in "For The Win" Perhaps one day Imgur will let us correct titles Until then, let's keep washing our hands, wearing masks & making LNI weird My mother, who is literally rough as fuck said she was hurting so bad the other night that she "wouldn't have cared if she died" W are all vaxxed & everyone but the teenager is boosted & have been staying too cautious, but it caught up with us anyway -hope the worst has passed for us & all of you as well https://www.newyorker.com/humor/daily-shouts/symptoms-of-the-new-variant-may-include

item.title: The kindness of a stranger made my day
item.description:
So, my fiancée has an old crown victoria that’s basically dead, and we’ve been looking for a car for her for about 3 weeks. In this crazy covid market it seems like everything is expensive, and it also sells out right away. I’ve had 5 different cars I wanted to go look at sell before I could get there. Plus, I’m an OBGYN resident, so I work about 70-80 hrs a week, and I can’t just leave early to go look at a car.
I had to borrow money to go to medical school, and even though I have a contract signed to make a lot of money later this year when I finish residency, the first 3 banks I called said my “debt to income ratio” was too high ?
I thought I had found “the car”, a nice Subaru Outback, low miles, fancy trim level… the ad said it was “super clean” but then I found out it was a salvage title for flood damage. I know enough from my time working on cars as a kid to know not to touch anything that’s been flooded. The electrical problems can haunt you forever.
Finally, I talked to a local credit union someone at work had suggested, and after talking to an actual human being (screw big banking btw), they said they could definitely get me a loan because they have lots of clients who are residents and understand the strange financial situation we’re in. Loan approved!!!
I have always loved minivans. When I was a kid, we were really poor, and some family friend gave us their old dodge caravan and we drove it all over the country. My dad and I hauled furniture, lumber, construction supplies, everything with that van. It was amazing. I have always wanted something I could use for road trips with my friends, and my fiancée and I do a lot of biking, and I would love to be able to just roll our bikes in the back of a van. Anyway, I decided to start browsing the ads for vans.
So… after days of scouring Facebook marketplace, I found a really nice dodge caravan, low miles, one owner, at a price that seems too good to be true. It was a solid $5k below market value. I contacted the seller and told him I needed a car for my fiancée, and he explained that it had been his wife’s car, but she had recently passed away and he didn’t need it any more. He asked me if I was a dealer because he said he wanted to sell the car to a person, not a dealer so he knew it would be well cared for.
I ended up talking mostly to the guy’s son, who told me his dad was a “car enthusiast” who had a bunch of hot rods. He said his father had meticulously maintained the van (synthetic oil changes every 3,000 miles, upgraded air filters new tires, the works), and his mother had really just used it to drive people around because she volunteered at an old folks home. I’ve bought my share of used cars, and I’ve had people lie to me before, but these people seemed legit so I begged them to hold the car for me and told them I’d come out with a check from the bank for their asking price the next day. It seemed too good to be true, but I didn’t want to miss a chance on a great car for my almost wife.
I was able to get approved for the loan, and I was planning to drive down with my fiancé to look at and just buy the car since it was about 2 hours away, and I only had Thursday off because I had just worked a 24 hour shift the day before. Unfortunately, the bank couldn’t get me the check till 3 and that meant we wouldn’t be able to go to the bank with the seller so he could confirm my check was real. I called him up to apologize for the delay and offer to meet another time and he said “it’s no problem, I trust you”. I even offered to let him look me up online to prove that I was a local doctor but he said “ son, I’m calling you from a flip phone. I wouldn’t know how to do that even if I wanted to”. ?
As I mentioned I’ve bought and sold my share of used cars, and done some wrenching in my day, and this was a beautifully maintained car. The owner told me he always replaced his air filters with KN filters so you didn’t have to keep buying new filters. He had always put the car on a battery charger in the winter and had just replaced the tires and battery. Even the glove box and the small compartments had clearly been cleaned and greased so that they opened smoothly. I was ready to hand him the check the moment I saw it. He let me and my fiancée take it for a test drive, and just handed us the keys. I asked him if he wanted to come with us and he said “no, it’s fine, I trust you”
We ended up buying the car of course. He handed me the title, walked me through the process of getting the car registered (I’ve never bought a car in this state since I moved for residency) and then after we had gotten a tour of his garage (he had a beautiful hot rod collection) and just talked about life for a whiled, we shook hands and headed out. While we were walking out the door, he pulled me aside and handed me a $100 bill and said “take your wife out to a nice dinner on me”.
My fiancée and I get married in just 2 weeks. Between the craziness of wedding planning and dealing with family for her, and the spike in COVID which has just made my life at the hospital extra crazy, we have both been feeling the stress. By the time we got out there to look at the van, I hadn’t slept in 37 hours, I was exhausted and feeling so frustrated with the whole car buying process. I was so worried this was going to be “to good to be true” but instead, we met an honest guy, who gave us a great deal and just made our day with his kindness. The world needs more people like Dave!
Check out our sweet new van!!! If driving a big truck or a sports car means your compensating for some thing… does driving a minivan mean you’re super well endowed… with confidence? Edit: most viral!!! Send me stories of your good relationships or advice for our future marriage. I have 15 days before I’m a husband and I’m pretty sure I’ve got this. The peen goes in the bellybutton right?

item.title: It's My Cake Day! Here are Some of My Favorite Music-ie Gifs. Enjoy!
item.description:
No sound, just cute
Marimba Zelda Theme. What's not to love?
Spongebob Flappy Ears!
Proper grip, plant your feet, and squeeze (don't pull) the trigger.
The commitment to the bit, tho... Slaps abound!
I long for the day when Wintergaten finishes the Marble Machine X (or has he just dropped the X and is starting anew?) and goes on tour.
Not really music, but at least using an instrument?
I mean, it's the Wellermen, it still slaps
Okapi Tax? Not mine, but I did take the photo. I do love these guys. In the words of your old pall, Stinky Wizzleteats, "Think of the little creatures in nature, they don't know that they're ugly". But seriously, these half giraffe, half zebra, half horse moafs are beautiful! Have a beautiful day and be nice to each other and most importantly to yourself! FP Edit: OMG! my very first FP post!! I ask for nothing as I am wont for nothing but what I hope I have already given you: a good end to your day and maybe just a little more joy than what you had before viewing this post :D
item.dc:creator: davmaldo

item.title: Holy Smoke... (Imgur Guide to how belief is changing)
item.description:
Religion is changing. By a host of metrics, across the world, we are seeing a shift in how we believe, and what we believe, and how we express it. And the implications are fascinating, if not a tad scary. A single Imgur post couldn’t hope to do this subject proper justice. Rather this is just a primer- a rough guide to the huge changes caused by the decline of many organised religions, why they are happening, and the diverse consequences this is causing around the world. You want more detail? Follow the links below to see the raw sources and feel free to read the data and come up with your own conclusions. This post covers a lot of ground, but at its heart is the implications of these changes, and how they weirdly seem to be impacting into every aspect of our lives, and maybe explain why we are seeing groups like QAnon suddenly emerge. Grab a coffee Imgur... There is a tl;dr for those who can’t be bothered (scroll down to the cat tax). Most Viral Edit: thank you usersub for actually insightful observations and thoughts. And for upvoting a post without a single crab. Like... dudes. Above and beyond.
This post is built upon two postulations- the first? Traditional religious belief (expressed in the form of large organised religions) is in decline/changing, globally. The second? This decline/change of traditional religious belief is not necessarily a completely good thing. Now hold up! I ain’t gonna start saying we are all Godless heathens, condemned to burn in hell for living in great sin and debauchery (We should be so lucky!). I ain’t espousing any belief system and don’t care what anyone does believe or doesn’t. That doesn’t matter.
What DOES matter is that the power dynamic in regards to religion is changing. And maybe that statement makes you wanna high five a buddy and go ‘hell yeah’ and hey I’d join in... except... Except that in all of human history, in all such matters, things don’t ‘just’ happen. Whenever there is a change in any power dynamic (religious, economic or political), whenever power is lost, something else gains that power. These things cause ripples. And if we accept that organised religion has been this big ole geopolitical force for the last few hundred years, that means the ripples can, and will, have profound implications. Geopolitical change never passes without someone dying because of it. Usually a lot of someone’s.
Realpolitik is a brutal thing, evolutionary in its ferocity. If something gets weak, other somethings sense that, and either that thing adapts or the other things take its place. So it really don’t matter if you are a member of an organised religion, or a confirmed atheist, or somewhere in between, the decline of organised religion is going to have an impact and simply, while some of the consequences of that impact will be good, some will be bad. That’s all. Usually, when you point this out, someone folds their arms and sulkily demands why do humans even NEED religion in the first place. It is actually a valid question. It’s worth asking. So why do we even have religions... no seriously?
Humans have, for the entire history of our species, believed in ‘stuff’. Being religious is something that appears in every human civilisation no matter how diverse they seem to be. We believe. That fact may annoy the hell out you but it’s there. We like to believe in religious things. Why? Well there are many reasons anthropologists, psychologists and historians can give and I really don’t want to get into the whole ‘it’s an evolutionary adaptation’ v ‘it’s an evolutionary by-product’ debate (which sounds like a trivial detail but trust me, it gets surprisingly intense) but there is one often overlooked, very pragmatic reason humans like to believe in something.
Mostly it’s because when we inevitably ask the question, “Why is my life so crap right now?’ We could reconcile the answer by going ‘Ah, this is the will of Yahweh/It is the judgment of Ohrmazd/My Karma score is screwed/I obviously insulted Mithras/my ancestors disapprove of my life choices’ or something like that and it allowed us at least make sense of the world. We humans seek purpose and religion has been a great way to give us this meaning. As the psychologist Johnathan Haidt once wonderfully said “Bird gotta fly, fish gotta swim and human being gotta be part of something larger than himself”. So, without taking a side in ongoing arguments about this very thing, the basic premise of this post is that humans have always liked to believe/belong to something that gave them meaning and purpose and because of that, religion and human civilization and politics, war, and all that other stuff tend to go hand in hand.
So what caused the ongoing decline? For the last 2000 or so years (give or take), the world has been dominated by some huge ass religions; Christianity, Islam, Buddhism, etc have been the underlying religious bedrock of the globe. But things have been changing. For a while now. To understand why some religions have declined we need to look at what differed between their high water mark and today (when the water is so low in some cases, we are wondering if there is a drought). And to understand that we need to understand a Latin word used by one sect to denote their authority.
Back in the day religions had a lot of non religious ‘magisteria’. Wut? It literally means ‘teaching authority’, and it is used to denote something you are THE expert on. Now if you want to talk to someone about the state of your soul say, or why evil exists, or some deep existential question (that could be summed up by simply asking ‘why is my life so crap right now?’), then finding a religion to address these issues with makes sense. This is their magisteria. But for the longest time religions (not just the Catholic faith in Europe) held magisteria over a heap of things that were not actually religious but because THEY were the intellectual bastions of education, folks believed it was like actual doctrine. Such as? Well religions were seen as the final authority on subjects as diverse as economics, architecture, law, science, war, art, politics, fashion, diet, agriculture, medicine, animal husbandry, navigation, philosophy, sexual habits, pharmacology, history and more. Both Christianity and Islam were bastions of vast realms of academic authority in things that had nothing to do with their traditional dogma.
Why was this? (Must resist the urge to go into insane historical detail about the early medieval period... must resist the urge...) Short answer: In Europe, academia and Christianity were the same thing for a long time. If you wanted to be educated, taught to read, and then go to places to find books to learn stuff from? You became a priest or a monk. With Islam? When the Muslims plundered the vast texts of the Middle East, Persian, India and the Hellenistic world, as they translated them into Arabic, their scholars began making connections between them and they saw massive advances in science, mathematics, medicine and more.
So, longgggg story short, for the past few hundred years we have seen the magisteria of religion shrink, as it returns to its core areas of expertise as they’d stopped being the only places you could learn this stuff. Obviously this has caused kick back over the centuries. Religions still try and claim these things, but the rise of stuff like modern capitalism, local planning laws, independent judiciary, the Geneva convention, all art since the Baroque, democracy, the global fashion trade, processed food, genetics, modern mass farming, SatNav, psychology, the pill, vaccines and secular universities across the world, means their authority is clearly diminished even if they complain about it. The process seems irreversible.
For example, no one finds it odd that religions and members of religious groups must obey the rule of law, right? But think about it- a body that was the manifestation of the will of God and as such was considered sacred, is now subservient to those societies taking something they consider MORE sacred (the law) and making Churches obey it. Big change. There is way, WAY more to this subject, and you could fill a whole library with the books just on this alone. The most crucial thing to realise is that for most of human history religion has been subservient to political forces; acting often as reinforcements of the status quo but occasionally as forces of rebellion or dissent (or both as in the case of Christianity in Rome). Not even seemingly monolithic ‘religious states’ like the Caliphate were immune to religion being twisted and turned as the powers that be dictated. It has been a powerful force sure, but there is no time when any religion was ever all powerful.
With the passing of years religion began to lose political power and intellectual power, but retained cultural power up until the last century, but over the key half century or so, we have seen genuine changes. The increased secularisation of education, the dilemmas of modernity, all have conspired to change how we see religion. As I said, there is stupendous amounts of detail I could go into here, and several competing rival theories as to the full causes (some of which I cover below) and rather than just go on and on about all the possible twists and turns, I’m just gonna focus again on what has happened in the last few years (as this is what is interesting me- today, here and now). TL;DR: The times they are a changin’ But as we said... change causes ripples.
So if religion as we knew it is changing and organised religion is on retreat, what is taking its place? Well NOT a period of enlightened skepticism wherein mankind transcends the need for religious beliefs. That would be nice but... (waits for it; crickets sound off in the distance; looks awkward). Rather, there is conflict. The change in belief is causing lot of conflict as others seek to exploit this for their own advantage. Now notice how I don’t say violence- the conflict isn’t always violent (but it can be). In many ways you could argue that the attempt by political powers to co-opt religious is not a new thing, but we are witnessing this play out differently in different parts of the world, with no two regions responding exactly the same way. So we need to look at a few examples of these changes in the interesting parts of the globe to try and see if we can judge the overall picture…
In China for example, religion is actually growing. Despite the CCP not allowing rivals to themselves, and Mao being exceptionally anti-religious, religion has grown in large numbers there, with an estimated 200 million believers of various faiths and denominations. Exact numbers are often hard to track as China accuses religious groups of inflating their numbers and they accuse China of deflating them. Some say the number could be as high as 350 million as added to that there is an undisclosed number who still worship ‘traditional folk religions’, as well as growing, untracked, membership of ‘underground’ and ‘house’ Christian churches (the first are Catholic and the latter are Protestant btw). Religion in China, however, seems to be following the path religion took in modern South Korea and Russia. A century ago, South Korea Christians became identified as the opposition to the Japanese occupation and for this reason, began growing in large numbers as they were seen as bastions of resistance. The Orthodox Church in the Soviet Union did exactly the same thing in regards to the Communist party there and played exactly the same role in the post-Communist Russia. In both cases, religion became a way for people to express mild or strong opposition towards the ruling hegemonic power.
This causes the CCP to blow hot and cold as they try and prevent religious groups from becoming a focus of opposition to the CCP but also desperately trying not to attack them all at the same time (in case in doing so they become a focus of opposition to the CCP). This is why we see President Xi clamp down on some religions while at the same time claim Confucianism, Buddhism and Taoism could help curb ‘moral decline’. Religion in China then is surprisingly NOT a force of societal oppression and control, but rather a force of rebellion, dissent and individuality. Being overtly religious in China is a small act of defiance. (Please note, the situation is not helped by the fact there are often confusing internal differences and tensions within China which can leave non-experts very confused. For example, China’s Muslim community has deep seated tribal and doctrinal differences. The majority Hui Muslim population flourishes, as opposed to minority Uyghur Muslims. The rivalries between those two communities literally go back centuries with both Hui and Uyghur committing pogroms upon the other, and today manifests itself with Uyghur often portraying Hui Muslims as ‘drug dealers’ (aka bad muslims and not ‘real ones’ like they are) and Hui portraying Uyghur as ‘tribal’ (aka using Islam to impose their tribal values on other Muslims).
By contrast, there is India, a nation that has prided itself as being a secular and democratic state, built upon tolerance of all religions for much of its history. It is the home of 94% of the world’s Hindus; but also the centre of Sikhism, Jainism and the largest concentration of Buddhists and Muslims on Earth. That’s a whole lot of religion... It’s always had religious extremists, but they never held power, and radical Hindu beliefs (Hindutva) were never a factor. In response to the horrific inter-community riots of 1993, however, there was a political swerve towards more conservative and nationalistic parties in elections, which began the rise of the Hindutva inspired BJP. After the 1996 elections, a low caste Hindu called Narenda Modi became chief minister of Gujarat; his personal charisma began his rise in national Indian politics.
As well as the BJP however, Modi was also a member of the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS), an ultra conservative Hindutva organisation devoted to preserving and elevating Hindu identity in India with the ultimate goal of recreating India as a Hindu state (hence their opposition to the traditionally secularist Congress party). With the BJP’s rise in power, Modi has been elevated to Prime Minister and made alliances with a broad range of like minded conservatives, and as such conservative nationalistic Hindutva ideals are spreading because it suits the political leadership to spread them. A union of Conservatism, Nationalism and Religion. This has empowered extremists and there have been a rash of attacks upon Mosques and Churches across India, with the lack of formal condemnation by the BJP causing criticism from more mainstream Hindu’s. Please be aware that Hindutva groups are ambitions. A campaign to redraw school textbooks with the ‘proper’ borders of India (which would include Nepal, Sri Lanka, Bhutan, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Afghanistan,Tibet and Myanmar inside them) started a while ago, and one hopes that never gets taken seriously...
The situation differs then from the situation in Sri Lanka and Myanmar. In those countries isolated Buddhist monks have capitalised upon fears of Muslims by painting a picture of the Buddhist way of life being under attack. This has caused the rise of violent militant Buddhist groups based upon charismatic individuals manipulating the fears of people. These groups while violent are usually small and don’t have any state sanction.... or didn’t anyway. Recently there seems to be official sanction of said groups by the Myanmar military junta. Not because they actually agree with those groups. It’s just the junta is so unpopular it will sanction anyone who will support them. Here is religion becoming on facet of a marriage of convenience between isolated extremists and unpopular military dictatorships.
In the Middle East however, religion seems to be everything amiright? Surely there can be no way I could be arguing that traditional religion is in decline there? Well, yeah... I kinda am. See Islam is having a hard time coping with modernity. Actually it’s been having a hard time coping with Modernity for the last couple of centuries but was kinda holding its own for a while there. But with ongoing geopolitical changes which began in 1979, Islam is facing a bit of a crisis. There are two things hitting Islam at the same time. The first is that in response to the complexity of the modern world, a small bunch of Muslims believe that folks should remove all ‘modern’ influences and get back to basics. And by basics they mean the 7th Century.
This medievalist belief (Salafism) would barely make any impact at all as its adherents are so pitifully small in number, but their willingness to murder anyone who disagrees with them indiscriminately, has made them stand out. Salafism drives groups such as Al-Qaeda and ISIS and while they hate the West for literally creating the modern world, they are at war with all other Muslims for daring not to agree with them. And at war with each other for exactly the same reasons. But Salafism, with its terrorist atrocities that grab our attention, is not the real force that is hitting the Muslim world. That is something different. Islamism. Islamists are the name given to members of a movement which began in Egypt but has spread across the Muslim world. It is basically Islam not as a religion, but as a political party. One you can vote for.
From Algeria to Indonesia, from Bangladesh to Palestine, Islamists campaign on being the Ultra-Conservative ‘saviours’ of Islam (confusingly some can also be the ultra-socialist ‘saviours’ of Islam). If elected to power they seem to almost universally try and then ban all future elections, and some seem to end up eventually using intimidation as their principle electioneering tool. This is not because Islamism is inherently violent; its just down to the irrafuatable reality that Muslims on the whole do NOT support Islamist parties, unless they are using them as a way to express displeasure at the rulers of their nation. Not being popular really infuriated some Islamists. https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/articles/do-muslims-vote-islamic/ The real power of Islamist movements however is that they tend to change the political debate, as traditional parties get worried they are losing votes for not being ‘Muslim enough’ and so swing right politically to target their base. We see this pattern copy itself in Algeria, Iraq, Bangladesh, Indonesia and more. And yet, in Muslim nation’s where democracy is embedded, this process has worked the OTHER way and we have seen Islamist parties water down their message to gain more votes. And it is worth saying that Iran is what you get when you place a Shia version of Islamism (Khomainism) in charge.
Also, it is the fear of Islamism’s influence on democracies which the feudal Muslim states then use as their justification for opposing democratic reform (see the UAE for example), but it is worth embracing the fact that as the above source demonstrates, most Muslim Democratic nations are, at their core, ‘culturally conservative and politically liberal’. In the Middle East then, nations are trying to balance political reality while the dominant religion is engaged in a deep seated series of theological debates as it comes to terms with the 21st Century... ...a process made more complicated by groups who believe the only way to conduct a deep seated series of theological debates is to kill everyone who disagrees with them.
Meanwhile the total opposite appears to be happening in sub-Saharan Africa and South America. The number of people who are members of organized religions has actually grown and seems set to continue. Indeed some talk of South America and Africa becoming the new ‘Christendom’ as they become the center of the faith in the decades to come, a reality suggested in the fact that we have a South American Pope and some of the contenders for his successor are African. It is worth noting that in South America the number of Catholics has dropped recently... and the number of Protestants has increased by the exact number. The number of faithful has remained, it’s just the flavour of faith they changed. Here then, religion seems to be remarkably unchanged, fulfilling its traditional roles but it will be interesting to see if the predictions of this region's dominance manifests itself. In particular the impact upon the Catholic Church could be profound given the large number of married African priests that exist in a ‘don’t ask, don’t say’ state of limbo and are breaking away because of this.
This leads us to Europe, where the decline in organised religions is most clearly seen. But aside from outliers like the PiS party in Poland copying the BJP in India and identifying Catholicism with nationalism and doubling down by banning abortion (a move which has backfired upon the Catholic Church in a huge way), the conflict seems over. (Shout out that Poland isn’t alone; across Eastern Europe the rise of nationalism mixed with old time religion is a thing; but it’s not some grand sweep of a bunch of religious groups in all these counties gazing at each other and going ‘Roll Tide’; more a mixture of Conservative/Popularism forces wearing religious clothing like a straight butch guy deciding to wear drag). Yet elsewhere in Europe, the decline of organised religion has NOT seen a huge rise in atheism. While numbers attending churches has fallen in countries like Ireland, Britain, Spain, France, Germany etc, there has been a corresponding rise in both in New Age beliefs and ‘unaligned spirituality’. Like South America a drop in one group leads to an increase somewhere else. And this is highly significant. The evidence suggests these changes have been caused by two powerful forces that have rocked traditional religions a lot. Syncretism and Sacralisation. Allow me to explain…
The Sacred and the Profane While we like to think that religions and faiths are these complicated things, at their heart is something way more simple and base and universal. Things we consider sacred. Sacred things are the bedrock upon which most faiths build out from. And the thing is... in today's world, sacred places are not always religious places. In fact as time goes by, and traditional religions influence decreases, the number of sacred things and places and ideas seems to be increasing. A great example of this are the various Tombs to the Unknown Soldier. In many countries, even secular ones, these monuments have become sacred places. They are revered and even the simple act of guarding them and ceremonies held at them, have become highly ritualized. They are sacred in all ways, showing you don’t need a religion to make such places.
Another example is the way some Americans treat their flag. The daily ritual that many Americans undergo of swearing allegiance it, means that for some the flag itself becomes a quasi-sacred item, and they get deeply upset at profaning the flag, by say, burning it (even if they often see no issue in wearing said flag on their ass). Making something sacred means we imbibe that place or that idea, with deep emotional feelings; as Haidt says, “When you sacredise something, you lose the ability to think clearly about it” (which may explain why folks can get upset about burning an American flag but then have no issue with using the flag as underwear). New Age beliefs allow its followers to create a whole new strata of sacred things, (veneration of the planet planet for example). But, as the above examples show, you don’t need an organised religion to do such things. Secular forces can now make things, places and ideas sacred.
Syncretic Blending Syncretic beliefs are the other thing we are seeing grow and which are challenging the power of organized Religion. Syncretism is a ‘pick and mix’ approach towards religious belief, manifesting itself with increasing numbers loosely defining themselves as ‘Christian’ say or ‘Jewish’, but who ignore parts of the doctrine they don’t agree with and refuse to affiliate with any organized church. This is a good thing I think. Within traditional religions, Syncretism is a powerful foil to Sectarianism, it doesn’t get hung up on the small print, and can allow you retain a faith but expel parts of the dogma you find objectionable. It allows you to define the boundaries of your faith as you see fit.
In many ways Syncretism is the ultimate manifestation of our modern world; a bespoke individual belief system; without any central authority telling you are wrong. A unique personal relationship with the universe/God/whatever. You can call yourself a ‘christian’ but not need a church; describe yourself as ‘spirtual’ and not need to define it; it’s your business, your beliefs, and that’s that. Syncretism and Sacralisation are powerful forces that have allowed personal and disorganized religious beliefs flourish not just in Europe, but in America also. And this brings us onto America which is the most fascinating place to examine the ongoing conflict dynamic.
The USA has seen a LOT of things all seemingly happen at the same time. American Evangelical Protestantism has allied itself firmly with the modern Republican Party in a way unprecedented in the countries history, producing a political situation identical to what we see in India or Poland or Russia; religious groups have done a deal with conservative politicians. At the same time, as attendances at their churches decline, Evangelicals seek to use politics as a way to regain or create new magisteria across a broad section of society, making previously non-religious matters things they can have a religious position upon, with issues as diverse as gender, capitalism, private property, respect for authority, the police, the military, wealth, the Founding Father’s, freedom of speech, and above all science (including not just biology but now increasingly focused on medicine) as things they claim authority over (or try to). This explains why Evangelicals support clearly objectionable leaders like Trump- while the focus is on the man, the actual prize is what he can give them.
In a broader sense, this alliance has caused a counter reaction, and this together with secular societies ability to sacralising everything, means that political debates are now filled with a moral dimension that changes their tone immediately. Ideas like kneeling during the anthem, or you opinion on the transgendered, immediately become issues of ‘good’ and ‘evil’. When adherents of this old morality run into adherents of the new morality, they get shocked. To the old guard they alone should be deciders of right and wrong. When called up by strict moralists on the other side who describe casual racism, ignorant misogyny and traditional power structures as ‘evil’, ‘patriarchal’ and ‘privileged’ the response is to either reject outright the counter morality and/or claim you are the victim of a ‘cancel culture’.
Sacralisation means however that no one is able to think clearly on such issues, all issues become highly charged and moderates on all sides are left blinking going ‘wait... is it just me? Isn’t everyone being REALLY intense about all of this?’ But as well as secular societies getting hold of sacred places, the impact of Syncretism upon secular beliefs has also taken place, and we could talk about political fragmentation, (and other side effects of this), but we need to pay close attention to one area in particular. Conspiracy theories.
You cannot help but notice how there are so many conspiracy theories these days. Seemingly about everything. The term ‘false flag’ has entered the common lexicon, and the tone of them is getting darker. Syncretism (that definition of modern belief) means people can mix and match their conspiracy theories, taking parts of some and adding them to another, to create new narratives. The current academic term for this is millennial conspiracist discourse and it’s a thing. A thing that is being studied and is causing some worry as the beliefs are getting more extreme.
But what happens when you mix the Syncretism of millennial conspiracist discourse with Syncretism of religion? Well, you end up with Pakistan. I ain’t kidding. For years my pet project had been watching how Islamist and Diobandi Muslim groups in Pakistan have used conspiracy theories to create a political-religious narrative about events going on around them. Pakistan became the home of the conspiracy theory for a few years back there. Here is the best example. In 2014 members of the TTP (a Salafist group closely allied with the Taliban) attacked a school in Peshawar murdering over 140 children. This act was condemned across the country and was reviled literally by every single Pakistani. https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-30491435.amp. Within days the theory arose- these were not Muslims who did this. They were CIA agents and this was a False Flag operation. Seriously. https://punarnavbharat.wordpress.com/2014/12/17/cia-false-flag-attack-on-peshawar-school/
Which is odd. As around the same time we also had people in America insisting that the CIA carried out 9/11. America has now become the home of conspiracy theories by the way. But... but hey, at least these conspiracy theories are not religious conspiracy theories right? Alas the lines are blurring and these blurred lines are where we start seeing much bigger and profound changes into how religion works going into the 21st Century.
Millennial conspiracist discourse and new religions First things first, within even the most well established traditional religious groups there are always to be found fringe belief systems based upon ‘conspiracy’ ideas. You know... Christian groups who believed in ancient secret races of giants; groups who believed in secret codes hidden in their holy books; groups who believe the apocalypse is gonna happen any day NOW.... Usually they remained small as you know, for every one of them there were about 100,000 of the mainstream faith who were all like ‘that’s just weird dude’. At times however organised religions would run with these conspiracy theories (‘Catholic’s are planning to overthrow us all’ and ‘Jews kill Christian babies’ being horrific past examples). But with the rise of Syncretism it means such groups don’t need to be part of mainstream faiths anymore.
And then when you take this and add it to New Age beliefs (who have never had a central authority to begin with)? Well you begin seeing strange things happen- New Age belief followers now ally with anti-Science Creationist Christians to say modern medicine is untrustworthy; die hard anti-socialist Republicans suddenly find themselves sprouting hard-core New Age anti-big corporation dogma but only about companies who are not right wing. But where the changes in religion and millennial conspiracist discourse really come together is around those people whose faith is built around belief in aliens. Now this is where it gets scary.
Firstly, I gotta say, I, like most folks, don’t have an issue if you believe in aliens and build a church about it. Mainstream religions can believe stuff just as weird/funky, and I’m an ex-Astrobiologist, so yanno... aliens are cool. Whatever dude. But belief in aliens always carries something of conspiracy theory built into its DNA it seems. Sometimes it’s about hidden powers the aliens gave us, like the Scientologists, the group created pulp sci-fi writer L. Ron Hubbard, who believe that by participating in very expensive tests you can unlock those powers; such groups tend not to be political at least even if they believe everyone is out to get them.
Sometimes they are groups who believe alien attack is imminent, like Falan Gong, created by Chinese business man Lin Hongzhi, who like to downplay the alien stuff, and emphasise their syncretistic merger of Chinese folk religions. They only partake in conspiracy theories for regional political reasons- like the belief the last US presidential election was stolen is used in one place, while some other claim about China is used in another. And sometimes it’s small obscure cults whose conspiracy theories are so intense that after twenty years being isolated from the world, all decide ‘this is THE moment’ and they literally kill themselves when an obscure comet flies overhead (the Heavens Gate Cult in 1997).
But where stuff gets dark is with what really started with the work of one man. David Icke is not a cult leader. He has no temple, has no followers, he’s just a writer and his claim to fame is that he has managed to corner a market in belief in aliens. He has created a highly successful narrative that is pure Syncretism of science fiction (in particular the 80’s show V and The Matrix), New World Order conspiracy theories and a few smatterings of New Age belief. He stole from other conspiracy theories (such as the ones that say the Illuminati secretly controls the world; the moon landings were fake; and that 9/11 was a false flag) and he placed them within his own twisted narrative of secret cabals of Lizard aliens in human skin, who manipulate mankind, and trap us in a false reality. This is a highly successful mixture of anti-semantic nonsense, anti-New World Order rants, ultimately places the blame of all the world's problems upon the rich elites.
What makes his beliefs (and the beliefs of the multitude of copycats) so dangerous is that it was Icke who combined modern conspiracy theories with religious belief in aliens to create something way more powerful. See he stumbled upon a very old weapon, one that organised religions had tended to hide away, and one which can be immensely dangerous. Theodicy. Theodicy is a very precise theological term; literally it means ‘God's Justice’ but it’s much more complicated than that. I could seriously get into massive detail on this, but it’s heavy going, so I’m just gonna simplify what it means and watch as theologians just stare at me in horror with my attempt. Theodicy is the name given to any belief system that says supernatural evil exists but the divine (God, the Goddess, Buddha, Allah, whatever) is ultimately a force for good and the good WILL triumph and you will be rewarded.
So why am I talking about this? Because those who study the quasi-religious groups on the fringes of mainstream beliefs have noticed that a growing number of groups have began using Theodicy. As David G. Robertson said in his book “UFO’s, Conspiracy Theories and the New Age: Millennial Conspiracism” “The function of millennial conspiracist discourse, however, is that by proposing the existence of an occluded malevolent agency, it explains the disenfranchisement – culturally, economically and crucially, epistemologically – of its subscribers. In short, millennial conspiracism provides a theodicy of the dispossessed.” A theodicy of the dispossessed. That should terrify any sane person. Why? What am I saying?
I’m saying that here is a belief system, unregulated, unorganised, which is without rules or leaders, a syncretic one which means you can believe the bits you want and ignore the rest, that empowers you to make whatever you want to become sacred or condemn whatever you wish as profane; which allows you become an expert on it, to claim your own magisteria on this topic, so everyone else can be ignored as ignorant or ‘sheeple’ and which says... “Why is your life so hard? It’s OK. It’s not you. It’s THEM. THEY control the world. Why do things seem crazy? THEM. Why are you poor? THEM. THEY are not even human. The rich work for them. The media works for them. THEY control the world.” See? A vague, non centralized ideology and belief system that validates your unhappiness, gives you a (hidden) supernatural force to blame and then allows you to identify specific elements of your society to be agents of these forces (and unlike previous examples of religions hating on folks, YOU as an individual can decide who this is). This is why they are dangerous.
And unlike Salafists say, or Hindutva beliefs, there is no central structure, no leaders to blame and condemn. Unlike Islamism there is no doctrinal core to use to oppose them, no ‘mainstream’ group to say ‘not us’. They are a self defined, self-contained whole, whose belief systems gives the world meaning and focus and above all, a reason to fear/hate everything about the modern world. The technical name for this is Post-Millennialist Alien Belief. But where this all gets EVEN MORE scary is when you mix all of this in with supposedly secular political beliefs. Because that allow great cross-fertilisation/Syncretism; this means you can take the basics above and replace ‘Lizard Aliens’ with ‘Child Molesting Billionaire Satanists’ say; or even ‘Big Business’ or ‘Big Pharma’ if you feel like it, and you can have the same Theodicy with your own pseudo-religious dogma.
And what happens when this wild, unregulated Theodicy runs into a large body of disenfranchised people suffering from what they feel is a lack of agency as the world spins seemingly beyond their ability to comprehend let alone control? You get QAnon. https://www.nytimes.com/article/what-is-qanon.html You end up with QAnon and thousands convinced it is their moral imperative to attack the Capitol building. Remember the danger from Post-Millennialist Alien Belief is not the actual bit that includes the Aliens; rather alien belief is indicative of an entirely unregulated, wild ocean of ideas and concepts that can run into one another, steal from one another, add fuel to one another, and cause wildfire pseudo-religious and religious groups to emerge suddenly out of nowhere and, as in the case of QAnon, dominate the entire political agenda of a modern democratic nation
And, sure, if folks REALLY want to argue that QAnon is not a religious group and was entirely political, I can only reply by saying.... When you see about a hundred QAnon followers turn up to Dealey Plaza to await the resurrection of the dead son of a President murdered in that spot...call it for what it is. Religious hysteria isn’t the sole property of religion anymore. https://www.forbes.com/sites/nicholasreimann/2021/11/22/qanon-supporters-pack-site-of-jfk-assassination-in-hopes-jfk-jr-and-maybe-his-dad-will-return/amp/
We have had big, seemingly monolithic belief structures and all powerful organised religions around for so damn long we have become convinced this is how religion works- we forget that for most of human history religion/faith was simply the most easily manipulated tool of human agency. No one joins a religion for coldly logical reasons, it is our emotions that send us towards them. They allow us make sense of the world, they make us feel like we belong to something, they give us meaning. And as we have seen, unless you have something to replace this meaning and purpose, something that can make adherents feel as emotionally supported, unless we can at least acknowledge this is what we need, then we in no state to deal with the changes around us. And we must.
Because religion isn’t ‘special’; people join Salafist groups for the exact same societal/psychological reasons they get dragged into weird cults that believe in Lizard people for the exact same reasons they find Incel communities for the exact same reason they join groups who believe vaccines are bad for them and for the exact same reasons they honestly, fervently believed that storming the Capitol in January 6th 2020 was a morally good act. Do they join these groups because organised religion is failing? Or because we have nothing in our modern secular world TO give us meaning? Where success and failure is reduced to ‘how much do we earn?’ Where purpose is reduced to ‘whatever works for you’? I don’t know. Seriously... I’m just a dude who summarises complicated stuff written by academics. I’m sure everyone has an opinion, but answers? What AM I sure on?
As radicalisation passes out of the control of traditional religion, we would start to see people radicalised by belief in secret alien conspiracies commit suicide bombings; people who believe vaccines contain hidden microchips set themselves on fire in public spaces; and people who care about the treatment of animals become so extreme they become identified as terrorist groups. And all of these are not in the ‘this could happen’ category. They already have. https://www.nytimes.com/2021/02/24/us/anthony-warner-nashville-bombing.html https://thehill.com/changing-america/well-being/mental-health/587989-man-sets-himself-on-fire-after-angry-rant-about https://www.splcenter.org/fighting-hate/intelligence-report/2002/stop-huntingdon-animal-cruelty-threatens-terrorist-style-attack
Karl Marx once said ‘religion is the opiate of the people’. He was right. It can be. And it can be used to prevent the passions of the people running rampant. And this IS a bad thing and he was right to complain. But he never experienced that faith, unregulated and uncensored faith, can be a wildfire. And we live in an age where we don’t NEED big religious leaders to trigger such flames anymore. In fact, as we can see, they can erupt from below us, religious hysteria tapped by secular forces. If that is the case, then the fires lit by QAnon were only the first, and the smoke it made is but a harbinger of more fires to come. TL;DR- the forces that drive religious hysteria have never been the ‘property’ of religion. With the decline in the hegemonic power of traditional religion, these forces are now being utilised by folks all over the world...
Sources: So many sources. Everything above isn’t MY theories. So if you wanna attack me, go for it. You are actually arguing with these guys. I am not a journalist dudes- I show my working outs... https://www.cfr.org/backgrounder/religion-china The CFR’s detailed overview of religion in China. Good resource. https://theconversation.com/amp/theres-a-religious-revival-going-on-in-china-under-the-constant-watch-of-the-communist-party-164211 Published last year a nice overview of the religious revival in China. https://www.aljazeera.com/features/2012/10/12/the-harsh-reality-of-chinas-muslim-divide There are many guides to the divisions within the Muslim community in China; I decided to go with one written by a Muslim news agency. https://wp.nyu.edu/compass/2019/12/08/negotiating-muslim-and-hui-identities-mosques-policies-the-entanglement-of-religion-and-ethnicity/ A brilliant, but very long and detailed, guide to the complexities of Islam in China; a subjective but wonderfully honest account of examining the realities of being Hui in China today. Fascinating stuff. https://thediplomat.com/2014/08/a-tale-of-two-chinese-muslim-minorities/ From way back in 2014, which showed the differences in treatment between the Hui and Uyghur and crucially kinda says China is probably gonna get more oppressive on one group and not the other. https://www.asianstudies.org/publications/eaa/archives/the-rise-of-hindu-nationalism-and-its-regional-and-global-ramifications/ Very detailed account of the rise of the Hindutva political movement in India; also why ultra-Conservative India’s supported Trump in such large numbers. https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-polisci-082112-141250 A hugely detailed assessment of what Islamism means and the vast range of explanations as to its causes and origins. I seriously disagree with some parts here BUT its a heck of a good resource. And an idea as to the greater depth of discussion about Islam going forward. https://www.pewforum.org/2015/05/12/americas-changing-religious-landscape/ From 2015- the study which exposed the huge wave of people defining themselves as unaffiliated in the United States; by comparing 2007 with 2014 we go to see the impact of the changes discussed above as they first hit the United States. https://www.pewforum.org/2021/12/14/about-three-in-ten-u-s-adults-are-now-religiously-unaffiliated/ From last December... and the trends identified in the 2015 report have carried on. While still a minority the growth of religiously unaffiliated in America continues. Also much more juicy details on how faith is changing in the USA. And how in some parts it ain’t. https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2021/12/13/key-facts-about-the-religiously-and-demographically-diverse-states-of-india/ Some baseline facts about religion in India. https://www.google.com/amp/s/mobile.reuters.com/article/amp/idUSKBN2A30SN How the recent swing towards Catholic Nationalism and hard line Catholic political positions by the Polish government is causing a decline in affiliation and attendance in Polish Catholic Churches. (Sorry about the link- the direct link to Reuter’s isn’t working on this story) https://www.pewforum.org/2014/11/13/religion-in-latin-america/ Much more in depth guide to religion in South America; fascinating insight to the most Christian part of the world. https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2018/08/22/the-worlds-most-committed-christians-live-in-africa-latin-america-and-the-u-s/?amp=1 Shorter overview but showing the growth in hard-core Christianity in Africa. https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/africa/a-more-open-way-of-being-catholics-in-kenya-rebel-against-celibacy-vow-for-priests/.html The Celibacy crisis for Africa’s Catholic priests; a really interesting debate to observe. https://www.lowyinstitute.org/the-interpreter/myanmar-extreme-buddhist-nationalists Fascinating and detailed look at the relationship between extremist Buddhists and the Myanmar military junta. http://content.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,2053761,00.html Falan Gong and Aliens: look, don’t take my word on it. The Time interview with the founder of the cult. Including; “The aliens have introduced modern machinery like computers and airplanes... Everyone thinks that scientists invent on their own when in fact their inspiration is manipulated by the aliens... The ultimate purpose is to replace humans.” https://news.sky.com/story/who-is-david-icke-the-conspiracy-theorist-who-claims-he-is-the-son-of-god-11982406 Short bio of David Icke for those who don’t know this guy. https://www.theguardian.com/media/2006/jan/22/broadcasting.observermagazine Some choice quotes by David Icke in an interview without any narration from the interviewer. Yeah... https://www.bbc.com/news/av/uk-politics-36339298 The ONLY link I will include to David Icke speaking. From an interview he gave, back in 2016 on a BBC politics show. About 3 and a half minutes in he gets asked about 9/11 and quickly moves onto the royal family are lizard aliens (and complains he can’t give the full backstory). Hard to believe this is one of the most influential New Age writers on Earth. https://warontherocks.com/2014/03/four-pakistani-conspiracy-theories-that-are-less-fictitious-than-youd-think/ A nice guide into the wild and wacky world of Pakistani conspiracy theories; this one is gentle. It explains how some of the less insane ones are inspired by US policy. Books: https://www.bloomsburycollections.com/book/ufos-conspiracy-theories-and-the-new-age-millennial-conspiracism/ Robertson’s book “UFO’s, Conspiracy Theories and the New Age: Millennial Conspiracism” is a fascinating study into the shifting beliefs towards Aliens and its crucial role in shaping conspiracist discourse. https://books.google.com/books/about/The_Spiritual_Revolution.html?id=RPSnu0zO-oEC Paul Heelas and Linda Woodhead et al. ‘The Spirtual Revolution’ (2009) was one of the more interesting books into the rise of ‘spiritualism’ (their name for individualist approaches to religious experiences), which, if verified, contains theories which explain the rise of New Age religions at the expense of Congregationalist religions. This was a fun post to write but a hard one. I wanted to try and articulate the vast changes we are seeing around us, while also keeping it entertaining. That was my SOLE motivation in this. I don’t have a religious or political agenda to get across. I do this stuff because I enjoy doing so, I get really interested in the world, and share what I find with others in the hope they too find it interesting. Because others have asked in the past, if you like what I did, and feel inclined AND can afford it, feel free to buy me a coffee if you think it warrants it. https://www.buymeacoffee.com/Thefeck If you can’t or don’t want to, then feel free to share your thoughts below as a kind word goes a long way.
item.dc:creator: thefeckamisigningupwithfacebook

item.title: This just in, it is Friday my dudes!
item.dc:creator: TheePerfectComment

item.title: He Is Good At Thinking
item.dc:creator: jrobertdobbsjr
